title: About the LL152 Guidance Desk
summary: LL152 Guidance Desk is a virtual team publishing source-checked NYC Local Law 152 practical guidance for owners, supers, and managers.
hero: Use the checker to narrow the likely branch first, then verify the final filing step against current DOB guidance before you file.
imageAlt: Dense New York City apartment buildings at dusk.

sections.title: What this project does
paragraphs: LL152 Guidance Desk is a narrow NYC Local Law 152 guidance site. It is built for owners, landlords, supers, and managers who need the next filing step to get clearer fast.
The site stays focused on three questions: does LL152 likely apply, which timing window matters now, and which gas-status branch changes the filing step.
bullets: Likely applicability and timing guidance
No-gas-piping versus no-active-gas-service guidance
Penalty, waiver, extension, and after-inspection context

sections.title: How to use it
paragraphs: Run the checker first when you have an address, BIN, BBL, property class, or district hint.
Use the intake only when one missing fact still changes the answer, due window, or filing sequence.
bullets: Use likely language until you verify the live DOB guidance.
Use the intake to share the blocker, not as proof that a filing rule is final.
Contact: shinhyeok22@gmail.com

title: How the LL152 verdict flow is built
summary: The checker is designed around official-source-first guidance, phase-one launch constraints, and language that stays specific without pretending to give legal certainty.
hero: The goal is to reduce filing ambiguity, not to overstate certainty.
imageAlt: Tall buildings with repeating windows in Manhattan.

sections.title: Editorial rules
paragraphs: The public copy uses likely, verify, and based on the current DOB guidance language on purpose.
The site does not claim guaranteed compliance, legal advice, or universal penalty outcomes.
bullets: Official DOB guidance outranks secondary plumber pages.
No gas piping and no active gas service remain separate scenarios.
Held pages stay noindex until evidence supports promotion.

sections.title: Operational rules
paragraphs: Phase 1 prioritizes the checker, next-step guidance, the no-gas split, penalty context, after-inspection timing, and the 2026 timing page.
Support pages remain available for internal or contextual use, but they should not displace the main verdict surface.
bullets: Source freshness status blocks promotion.
CTA clicks and lead submissions are recorded by route family.
Admin summaries are file-backed in phase 1.

title: Contact the LL152 Guidance Desk
summary: Send case details when one unresolved fact still changes the filing path, due window, or gas-service branch.
hero: Send the building facts you already have. The shared inbox keeps the open question, building reference, and contact details together so the next reply starts from the same case context.
imageAlt: Office desk with a city view in the background.

sections.title: When to contact now
paragraphs: Contact now when one missing fact can still flip the likely filing path, due window, or gas-status branch.
The goal is to keep the case context together before a wrong assumption sends the building into the wrong filing sequence.
bullets: The building seems covered but the exact next filing step is still unclear
No gas piping versus no active gas service is still unresolved
The due window may already be live or already missed
DOB, DOF, or utility records conflict with what you think the building is

sections.title: What to send
paragraphs: Share only the facts that still change the answer.
If you already know the address, BIN, BBL, district, or gas status, include it directly so the next reply starts from the current blocker instead of from zero.
bullets: Address or building identifier
Gas piping status and active service status
The filing, timing, or certification question blocking the next step
Contact email: shinhyeok22@gmail.com

title: How building details and contact data are handled
summary: Use this page to understand what the intake asks for, where the current CSV workflow stores it, how long it is kept, and how to request deletion or correction.
hero: Share only the building facts needed to clarify the next LL152 step. Keep DOB credentials, payment data, government IDs, and emergency reports out of this intake.
imageAlt: Desk workspace with a laptop, notebook, and phone.

sections.title: What the intake stores
paragraphs: The intake stores the name, email, phone, building address or identifier, page path, scenario context, and the message you submit.
The current phase-one workflow keeps that data in CSV files so the page context and the follow-up note stay together.
bullets: Name, email, and optional phone number
Building address or identifier
Page path, intent, and scenario context
A short message about what still blocks the next step

sections.title: Retention, correction, and deletion
paragraphs: The intake is meant for lightweight case handling, not for long-form document storage.
If you need a correction or deletion request, email shinhyeok22@gmail.com from the same address you used in the intake.
bullets: Keep submissions limited to what changes the answer
Use email for deletion or correction requests
Do not send large attachments or portal credentials through the intake

sections.title: What to leave out
paragraphs: Do not send DOB NOW credentials, payment details, government IDs, or unrelated tenant information through this form.
The intake is for routing context, not for account takeover, billing, or emergency gas reporting.
bullets: No DOB passwords or login screenshots
No credit card or bank data
No Social Security or tax identification data
No emergency condition reports that should go directly to 911, your gas utility, or DOB emergency channels

title: What happens after you send case details
summary: The intake keeps the likely next step, blocker, and contact details together so the next reply can start from the current case context.
hero: Use the intake for non-emergency LL152 questions. It does not replace DOB filing, utility emergency reporting, or licensed sign-off.
imageAlt: Team workspace with notebooks and a city office view.

sections.title: What the intake is for
paragraphs: The intake is meant to keep the likely next step, the missing fact, and the building reference together in one place.
Use it when the blocker is still a filing-path question, not when the remaining work is simply uploading a known form.
bullets: Likely coverage versus exemption framing
Due-window and district context
No gas piping versus no active gas service guidance
Inspection, GPS1, GPS2, correction, waiver, or extension context

sections.title: What this intake does not do
paragraphs: The intake does not submit anything to DOB, guarantee that DOB will accept a filing, or replace licensed professional sign-off.
It is a routing aid for non-emergency LL152 questions.
bullets: Not DOB filing submission
Not emergency dispatch
Not legal advice or licensed sign-off
Not a promise that the final answer stays unchanged after official verification

sections.title: When to use official emergency channels instead
paragraphs: Do not wait for email if there is gas odor, an unsafe condition, or an immediate reporting obligation.
Use 911, your gas utility emergency line, or DOB emergency reporting when the issue is urgent.
bullets: Gas odor or active gas emergency: call 911 and your gas utility emergency line
Unsafe conditions requiring immediate reporting
DOB submission tasks already due now
Any situation where licensed professional sign-off is the immediate blocker

title: Terms, disclosure, and usage boundary
summary: This site publishes informational LL152 guidance. It is not legal advice, not a government service, and not a guarantee that DOB will accept any filing.
hero: Use the site as a source-checked filing aid. The final filing step still has to be verified against current official guidance and any licensed sign-off requirement.
imageAlt: Documents and a notebook on a work table.

sections.title: Informational use only
paragraphs: This site is built to reduce filing ambiguity around NYC Local Law 152. It does not replace the current DOB notice, DOB NOW instructions, or any required licensed sign-off.
Use the output as a guided starting point, then verify the final filing step before you submit anything.
bullets: Not legal advice
Not engineering advice
Not a guarantee that a filing is complete or accepted

sections.title: Operator and contact
paragraphs: The site is operated as a virtual guidance team under the name LL152 Guidance Desk.
Questions about the site, privacy, or submitted case details can be sent to shinhyeok22@gmail.com.
bullets: Virtual team name: LL152 Guidance Desk
Contact email: shinhyeok22@gmail.com
Independent project, not a government office

sections.title: Disclosure
paragraphs: If sponsored listings, partner referrals, or paid handoffs are added later, they should be labeled clearly on the page where they appear.
Until then, treat every page as informational guidance and verify the final action through the current official source set.
bullets: No hidden government affiliation claim
No claim that contact equals representation
No promise of outcome, acceptance, or deadline relief

title: This site is not government-affiliated
summary: The LL152 Guidance Desk explains likely next steps under the current DOB guidance, but it is not a government service and it does not submit filings for DOB.
hero: Use the site to reduce ambiguity, then verify the final filing path against the current official guidance.
imageAlt: Downtown city corridor between large buildings.

sections.title: Important boundary
paragraphs: This product does not replace official DOB notices, filing systems, or professional sign-off.
The public promise is narrow: explain the likely compliance next step and help the user keep the next action clear.
bullets: Not a DOB portal
Not legal advice
Not a guarantee that a filing is complete or accepted
Not a government-affiliated service

title: Does this building need LL152 inspection?
summary: Use the LL152 checker to see whether the building likely needs inspection, which cycle applies, and which filing path matches the gas status.
hero: Start with the smallest set of building facts you already know, then narrow the answer until the next LL152 filing step is clear.
imageAlt: Residential buildings lined up along a New York block.

sections.title: What the checker looks for
paragraphs: The fastest routing inputs are address or building identifier, building profile, district hint, gas piping status, and active gas service status.
bullets: Coverage and due-cycle hints
No-gas-piping versus no-active-gas-service split
Likely next filing or correction path

title: LL152 filing next step after inspection, GPS1, or GPS2
summary: Use this page when the building already looks covered and the open question is what gets filed next, who acts next, and which document starts the sequence.
hero: Once the building looks covered, the question is what gets filed next, who moves first, and which document sequence applies before DOB NOW.
imageAlt: Mid-rise buildings under a bright New York sky.

sections.title: Use this page when
paragraphs: The building is likely covered and the blocker is no longer applicability. The blocker is the next filing step, inspection path, or certification path.
DOB's current filing timeline is GPS1 to the owner within 30 days of inspection and GPS2 from the owner to DOB within 60 days.
bullets: You need to move from verdict to filing action
You need to know whether the gas-status split changes the paperwork
You need to know whether LMP help is now the right next action

sections.title: Use a different page when
paragraphs: If the building truly has no gas piping, or still has piping but no active service, the no-gas split page is usually the better guide before you act.
bullets: No gas piping goes to a different next-step path
No active gas service still needs filing-aware confirmation

title: LL152 no gas piping vs no active gas service
summary: Use this comparison page when you need to separate the two most common LL152 branch mistakes before you file or open DOB NOW.
hero: These are not the same answer. No gas piping and no active gas service change the signer, documents, and repeat-cycle obligation.
imageAlt: Exterior of a dense city block with mixed building types.

sections.title: No gas piping
paragraphs: Start here when the building truly has no gas piping. This is not the same as a building with dormant service.

Under the current DOB rule set, certification that the building has no gas piping is a one-time submission and may come from a utility company, registered design professional, or licensed master plumber.
bullets: Treat certification language carefully
Verify the current DOB guidance before assuming the submission type
Do not borrow the active-service filing path

sections.title: No active gas service
paragraphs: When gas piping still exists, the building often remains filing-sensitive even if service is not active right now.
DOB now requires utility-company documentation for this scenario every cycle, plus an owner statement that the building no longer receives gas service and no appliances remain connected.
bullets: Keep piping presence in view
Use filing-aware confirmation before you call the route resolved
Check whether correction or certification support is still needed

title: LL152 no gas piping certification: when the filing is one-time
summary: Use this page when the building truly has no gas piping and you need to know who can certify it, what to collect, and why the filing is different from no active gas service.
hero: This page only works when the building truly has no gas piping. If piping still exists anywhere, stop and move to the no-active-gas-service page instead.
imageAlt: Rooftop and upper-floor mechanical structures above New York apartment buildings.

sections.title: Use this page when
paragraphs: Use this page only when the building truly has no gas piping. This is a one-time certification path under the current DOB guidance, not a recurring-cycle no-service path.
This answer stops working the moment gas piping still exists anywhere in the building, even if service is not active.
bullets: No gas piping anywhere in the building system
One-time certification path under the current DOB guidance
Signer can be a utility company, registered design professional, or licensed master plumber

sections.title: What to collect before filing
paragraphs: Keep the building identity and signer details ready before you treat the answer as settled.
The point is to prove no piping, not just no active service.
bullets: BIN or BBL and exact building identity
Statement of the no-gas-piping condition
Signer identity and utility or license details
Any building notes that rule out a dormant-service scenario

sections.title: Use a different page when
paragraphs: Many wrong filings happen because a utility shutoff is mistaken for a no-piping condition.
If piping still exists, even as abandoned or inactive piping, this page is no longer the right fit.
bullets: Gas piping still exists even if service is off
Abandoned or hidden piping has not been ruled out
Utility shutoff is being confused with piping removal

title: LL152 no active gas service: what DOB still expects each cycle
summary: Use this page when gas piping still exists but the building no longer has active gas service and you need the recurring documentation path, not the no-gas-piping page.
hero: No active gas service does not mean no gas piping. When piping still exists, the answer stays filing-sensitive and usually needs utility documentation every cycle.
imageAlt: Dense Manhattan apartment facades in late afternoon light.

sections.title: Use this page when
paragraphs: Use this page when gas piping still exists in the building but the utility no longer supplies active gas service.
This is not the same as the one-time no-gas-piping certification path.
bullets: Gas piping is still present
Service is off or fully deactivated
The building needs recurring-cycle documentation, not one-time no-piping certification

sections.title: What DOB expects
paragraphs: This page usually needs utility-company documentation plus an owner statement that no appliances remain connected.
This answer stays recurring until the building condition changes.
bullets: Utility-company documentation for the service status
Owner statement that no gas appliances remain connected
Repeat the documentation every cycle until the condition changes

sections.title: What still needs confirmation
paragraphs: If shutoff dates, appliance removal, or the piping condition are still unclear, the answer is not stable yet.
At that point the problem is no longer a simple documentation question.
bullets: Utility shutoff dates are unclear
Appliance removal is still unresolved
The building may still need inspection, correction, or another filing path

title: LL152 penalty, extension, and waiver options
summary: Penalty questions usually hide the real next-step problem: what can still be filed, whether a waiver path exists, and whether correction or certification support is now required.
hero: A penalty question is rarely just a penalty question. It usually means the next filing step is still unresolved and needs to be tightened before you act.
imageAlt: New York avenue with buildings and traffic.

sections.title: What this page is for
paragraphs: Use this page when the issue is missed timing, open exposure, or confusion about whether an extension, waiver, or correction path is still available.
The current rule text and Cycle 2 notice split the civil penalty between $1,500 for a 3-family building and $5,000 for most other buildings.
bullets: Do not assume every late building shares the same outcome
The missed step still needs to be identified before the remedy is clear
Gas status can still change the correct next action
Current waiver grounds include ownership changes, misclassification, no active gas service, no gas piping work in progress, and other rule-based categories

title: LL152 GPS1 and GPS2 deadlines after inspection
summary: Use this page after the inspection when GPS1, GPS2, correction timing, or late submission is the blocker.
hero: After inspection, the work is not automatically done. The filing path still needs to match the actual inspection outcome and deadline sequence.
imageAlt: Rows of city buildings seen from an elevated viewpoint.

sections.title: Use this page after inspection when
paragraphs: Use this page when the blocker is no longer whether the building is covered. The blocker is the after-inspection sequence, timing, or correction path.
The baseline DOB sequence is GPS1 within 30 days, GPS2 within 60 days, correction certification within 120 days, or within 180 days when additional time was requested.
bullets: Inspection completed but next filing is unclear
GPS1 and GPS2 sequence confusion
Correction timing is the real blocker

title: LL152 2026 deadline by community district
summary: This page is for owners who think 2026 is the live timing window and need a next-step framing before they verify the exact sub-cycle or filing date against the current DOB notice.
hero: Treat this page as the timing guide for active-cycle intent, not as a substitute for the live DOB notice.
imageAlt: Wide New York skyline with high-rise buildings.

sections.title: What this page should answer
paragraphs: Use the page when the user is trying to confirm whether 2026 is the active year for the building and what the next filing step would be if it is.
DOB's current Cycle 2 map places community districts 4, 6, 8, 9, and 16 in Sub-cycle C for calendar year 2026.
bullets: Use district, BIN, or BBL to tighten the timing check
Verify the live DOB notice before submitting
Move to the gas-status split if the timing is clear but the filing path is not

title: Missed LL152 deadline: what to do before penalty gets worse
summary: Use this case page when the building should have filed already and the real question is whether to fix the missed step, challenge the notice, or prepare a waiver request.
hero: A missed LL152 deadline is not just a payment problem. First identify the filing or correction step that was missed, then decide whether penalty, waiver, or challenge is the real next move.
imageAlt: Busy New York street with yellow taxis and mid-rise buildings.

sections.title: Start here when
paragraphs: Use this page when the building looks covered, the original due window likely passed, and you still do not know whether the missed step was inspection, GPS2, correction certification, or something else.
The first job is to identify what was not filed or what was filed late before you decide whether payment, waiver, or challenge is the better move.
bullets: Confirm the notice date and the community district
List what has already been filed and what has not
Keep BIN, BBL, and any DOB notice together before you respond

sections.title: Do not assume
paragraphs: The penalty answer can change with building type, gas status, exemption issues, and whether the city record itself is wrong.
Three-family buildings and misclassified properties can turn into very different next steps from a typical larger building.
bullets: Do not treat every late case as the same $5,000 problem
Do not pay first if the property record still looks wrong
Do not skip the missed filing analysis just because the notice looks final

title: GPS2 correction after inspection: what gets filed next
summary: Use this case page when the inspection already happened and the open issue is GPS2, correction timing, or how to document follow-up work before deadlines stack.
hero: After inspection, the hard part is often not the inspection itself. It is knowing whether GPS2 is ready, whether correction work is still open, and which deadline now matters most.
imageAlt: Tall Manhattan buildings lining a dense city corridor.

sections.title: Use this page when
paragraphs: Use this page when the inspection already happened but the next filing move is still unclear.
Typical confusion here is whether GPS2 can be submitted now, whether correction work still controls the timeline, or whether a follow-up certification deadline is already running.
bullets: Inspection date is known but next filing owner is unclear
GPS1 was received but GPS2 is still open
Correction work may still be active after inspection

sections.title: What usually changes the answer
paragraphs: The answer often turns on whether the inspection resulted in safe conditions, correction conditions, or a missed filing entirely.
Keep the inspection date, the LMP output, and any correction scope together before you act.
bullets: Do not treat GPS2 and correction certification as the same filing
Do not assume the clock starts from the day you read the notice
Do not separate the inspection outcome from the filing deadline

title: Mixed-use building LL152 coverage: what usually decides it
summary: Use this case page when a building has residential and commercial use and the main question is whether the official property record still puts it on an LL152 filing path.
hero: Mixed-use labels alone do not settle LL152. The practical question is what DOB and DOF records say the building is, whether the property is exempt, and which filing step follows from that record.
imageAlt: Mixed-use New York block with storefronts below residential windows.

sections.title: Start with the record
paragraphs: Use this page when a building looks mixed-use in real life and you do not know whether that helps, hurts, or changes LL152 coverage.
The practical answer usually comes from the official property class, BIN, BBL, and the record DOB is actually using for the notice or filing path.
bullets: Check DOF class before relying on a broad building label
Keep BIN and BBL ready when records conflict
Treat street-level use and legal class as separate facts

sections.title: What makes mixed-use cases go wrong
paragraphs: Many owners assume the street appearance or lease mix answers the question by itself.
The better question is whether the official property record still points toward coverage, exemption, or a correction to the city record.
bullets: Do not assume mixed-use means automatic LL152 coverage
Do not assume residential units mean automatic exemption either
Do not ignore a notice conflict just because the building label sounds obvious

title: Gas shut off but piping remains: LL152 no active gas service case
summary: Use this case page when the building no longer receives gas service but piping is still in place and you need the recurring documentation path, not the one-time no-piping certification.
hero: Utility shutoff does not automatically move a building to the no-gas-piping side. If piping still exists, this usually stays a no-active-gas-service case and the documentation repeats by cycle.
imageAlt: Row of older city buildings with visible mechanical systems and facades.

sections.title: Use this page when
paragraphs: Use this page when the utility shut off service but nobody can honestly say the building has no gas piping anywhere.
That usually means the building still belongs on the no-active-gas-service side until the piping condition truly changes.
bullets: Service is off but piping still exists
Utility paperwork exists but piping removal does not
The question is recurring documentation, not one-time certification

sections.title: What to gather before filing
paragraphs: Keep the utility shutoff record, the building reference, and the owner statement together before you upload or submit anything.
This case goes wrong when a shutoff letter is treated as proof that piping no longer exists.
bullets: Utility documentation showing shutdown or deactivation
Owner statement that no gas appliances remain connected
Any facts showing piping still exists in the system

title: One- or two-family building got an LL152 notice: what to check first
summary: Use this case page when a building looks exempt as a one- or two-family property but DOB or DOF records still point you toward an LL152 notice, filing, or penalty.
hero: When an exempt-looking building still gets an LL152 notice, the first job is not to pay or file blindly. It is to confirm the official class, the record DOB is using, and whether the notice is aimed at the wrong property profile.
imageAlt: Low-rise residential houses on a New York neighborhood street.

sections.title: Check the record before acting
paragraphs: Use this page when the building looks like a one- or two-family property but the city notice still points you into LL152.
The practical question is whether the official DOF class, BIN, or BBL is wrong, outdated, or tied to a different property record than you expected.
bullets: Check DOF class before paying or filing
Compare the notice to BIN and BBL
Keep any exemption clue tied to the official property record

sections.title: What to keep ready
paragraphs: Record conflicts are easier to sort out when the property identifiers, the notice itself, and the claimed exemption basis are all in one place.
This is often a record-cleanup problem before it becomes a filing problem.
bullets: Notice copy or violation reference
BIN, BBL, and property-class reference
Any city record or prior filing that supports exemption

title: LL152 corrected certification: what gets filed after conditions were fixed
summary: Use this page when the inspection found conditions, the correction work is already done or underway, and the open question is what the corrected-certification filing actually requires next.
hero: A corrected certification is not the same step as GPS2. Use this page when the building already moved past the inspection result and now needs the follow-up correction filing path.
imageAlt: Sunlit Manhattan street with mid-rise buildings.
ctaLabel: Get help with this filing step

sections.title: Use this page when
paragraphs: Use this page when the inspection already happened, conditions were identified, and the main blocker is what has to be filed after the correction work is complete.
The practical question is usually whether the building is still waiting on work, ready for corrected certification, or already late because the post-inspection clock kept running.
bullets: Start with the original inspection date and condition status
Keep the LMP report and the correction scope together
Separate corrected certification from GPS2 before deciding what is due

sections.title: What usually changes the answer
paragraphs: Correction cases go wrong when owners treat every post-inspection filing as the same step.
The deadline, signer, and next move can change depending on whether the condition is already fixed, still being fixed, or needs a new inspection before certification.
bullets: Do not assume GPS2 closes the correction path
Do not assume a contractor invoice by itself is the final filing support
Do not wait on penalty notices to figure out whether corrected certification is already due

title: LL152 unsafe or hazardous condition: what happens immediately after inspection
summary: Use this page when the inspection did not end as a routine filing case because the condition may be unsafe, hazardous, or serious enough that the next move is immediate reporting rather than ordinary scheduling.
hero: An unsafe or hazardous LL152 result is not a normal follow-up page. Use this route when the inspection result may trigger immediate notice to the utility, DOB, and the owner before the regular filing sequence continues.
imageAlt: High-rise buildings across the New York skyline.

sections.title: Use this page when
paragraphs: Use this page when the inspection result points to an unsafe or hazardous condition and the next move is no longer just GPS1, GPS2, or a routine correction deadline.
The goal here is to separate immediate reporting obligations from the later paperwork so the case does not get treated like an ordinary compliance follow-up.
bullets: Unsafe-condition cases can trigger immediate notice requirements
Treat utility, DOB, and owner notice as distinct from later filing steps
Keep the inspection result and any emergency action details together

sections.title: What not to blur together
paragraphs: This page is not general emergency advice and it is not the same as every correction case.
The practical distinction is whether the condition requires immediate notification first, or whether the building is already back in a normal corrected-certification or GPS2 path.
bullets: Do not downgrade a hazardous finding into a normal timing question
Do not assume later filing deadlines replace immediate reporting duties
Do not lose the original inspection language when asking for help

title: LL152 exempt building notification: when the property record points the wrong way
summary: Use this page when the building looks exempt on paper, but DOB or DOF records still point the owner toward an LL152 filing, notice, or violation path.
hero: This route is for record conflict, not guesswork. Use it when the property profile appears exempt but the official city record still drives the building into an LL152 filing or notice path.
imageAlt: Apartment buildings viewed across a city skyline.

sections.title: Use this page when
paragraphs: Use this page when the building appears exempt because of occupancy or DOF class, but the city record or notice still treats it like an LL152 filing case.
The practical job is to verify the property record, not to assume the notice is right just because it was issued.
bullets: Use DOF class and official property-profile checks before assuming an exemption
Cycle 2 violation notices allow challenge or waiver requests within 30 days from the notice date
If a one- or two-family home is misclassified, use the exempt-building notification path rather than assuming the notice is correct

sections.title: What usually drives this page
paragraphs: Most exemption conflicts come from the property record, not from the owner's memory of the building type.
One- and two-family notices, mixed-use confusion, and R-3 style classifications are the recurring reasons this route matters.
bullets: Check the DOF class before deciding whether to file or challenge
Keep BIN, BBL, and the notice copy together
Do not pay a penalty first if the exemption question is still unresolved

title: LMP finder support route
summary: Broad finder pages stay held until the checker and next-step pages already show reliable routing demand.
hero: Do not let a broad directory replace the verdict product before the verdict product proves real demand.
imageAlt: Workspace with city skyline outside the window.

sections.title: Current status
paragraphs: This route is intentionally held and noindex. It should only move forward after the checker and next-step pages already prove genuine LMP-routing demand.
bullets: Not a public directory
Only promote after verdict routes show real routing value
